1.pericope_label: The Sabbath Rest for God's People
1.exposition: The chapter opens on a warning that is really an invitation. Because the promise of entering God's rest still stands unclaimed, the community must fear — not with a slavish dread, but with the watchful care of those who might yet fall short (v.1). The same good news that reached the wilderness generation has reached the readers, but bare hearing saved no one: the word must be mixed with faith (v.2). From there the writer mounts one of the New Testament's most intricate arguments about a single word. Scripture speaks of God's own rest at creation (Genesis 2:2); yet centuries after Joshua settled Israel in the land, David still holds out a “Today” and a rest not yet entered (Psalm 95). Therefore Canaan was never the true rest — a sabbatismos remains for the people of God (v.9), entered by ceasing from one's own works as God ceased from his (v.10). The conclusion is urgent, not academic: strive to enter that rest (v.11).
1.original_language: Two Greek words carry the argument. Throughout, the general word for rest is katapausis (a settling, a ceasing) — the term drawn from Psalm 95:11. But at the climax the writer coins a rarer one: sabbatismos (v.9), a “Sabbath-keeping,” found nowhere in Greek before him. English “rest” flattens the shift; the change of word signals that the rest that remains is not mere cessation but the deep, festal repose of God's own Sabbath, shared by his people. And the paradoxical imperative of v.11, spoudasōmen (“let us give diligence, strive earnestly”), holds the labour and the rest together in one word.
1.historical_context: Jewish tradition layered the idea of “rest.” There was the weekly Sabbath, a participation in God's creation-rest (Genesis 2:2) and the sign of the covenant (Exodus 31:16); there was the Promised Land, “rest from all your enemies” (Deuteronomy 12:10), which Joshua's generation received (Joshua 21:44); and there was the rest still to come, the world-to-come of which the Sabbath was a weekly foretaste. The writer joins Genesis 2:2 and Psalm 95:11 — both speaking of God's rest — by the standard rabbinic method of linking two texts that share a word, to prove that a rest beyond Canaan was always intended.
1.christ: The rest that “remains” is finally a rest in Christ. He is the one who, having made purification for sins, “sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high” (1:3) — the divine rest of finished work that every Sabbath foreshadowed. To enter his rest is to step inside his completed labour and cease from the futile toil of justifying oneself. As God's rest crowned the first creation, so Christ's rest crowns the new; entering it now, by faith, is a real foretaste of the consummation his death and resurrection secured.

verses.note: Paul draws the practical edge from Israel's fall: let us fear, lest — the promise of entering his rest still standing — any of you be judged to have come short of it. grammatical-historical Gill is careful about the fear: not a dread of wrath, nor a distrust of God's power and goodness, but a godly jealousy, a watchful circumspection. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the stress falls on fear, not on us — and that the writer, though he will say “any of you,” begins gently with “let us.” reformed Calvin adds that this fear does not shake the confidence of faith but keeps us from growing torpid in indifference.

The reason Israel's privilege profited nothing: the good news came to them as it has to us, but the word they heard did not profit them, not being mixed with faith. grammatical-historical Barnes weighs the metaphor of “mixing” — as food does the body no good unless it is digested and blended into the frame, so the message nourishes only the soul that receives it by faith. puritan Matthew Henry puts it plainly: faith in the hearer is the very life of the word, and unbelief the root of all unfruitfulness under it.

We who have believed do enter that rest — faith is the door, and unbelief the bar. grammatical-historical Gill presses that this is no merely future rest but a present, spiritual rest in Christ: a soul-rest from the wearisome labour of seeking righteousness by the works of the law. The oath “they shall not enter my rest” is a Hebrew form of solemn exclusion; and the added clause “though the works were finished from the foundation of the world” already reaches toward the next argument — God's own rest has existed since creation, yet that primal rest was not the whole of what was meant.

The first proof that a divine rest exists: God rested on the seventh day from all his works (Genesis 2:2). grammatical-historical Barnes pictures the rest of God himself — the Creator, his work finished, happy in the contemplation of what he had made, hallowing the day. Here is a “rest of God” spoken of from the very beginning, the pattern and root of all the rest that follows.

Set beside it, a second word: “If they shall enter into my rest” — spoken in Psalm 95, long after creation. grammatical-historical Gill marks the force: God still speaks of “my rest” as a thing not yet entered, so the creation-rest cannot exhaust the promise — a further, distinct rest remains to be gained.

It remains that some must enter it, and those who first heard the good news failed to enter through unbelief. grammatical-historical Barnes reasons from the certainty of God's promise: a rest is spoken of, therefore it must be enjoyed by some; and since those first invited forfeited it, the promise is not exhausted but reaches forward to a people who will believe.

The proof advances on a single word — “Today”. Long after Moses and long after the land was possessed, God in David still fixes a “Today” of opportunity: “Today, if you will hear his voice, harden not your hearts.” grammatical-historical Gill draws the inference — since the seventh-day rest was God's own and the Canaan-rest the unbelieving forfeited, there must yet remain both a people and a time to enter.

“If Joshua had given them rest, God would not afterward have spoken of another day.” grammatical-historical Gill clears the name: the “Jesus” of the older versions is Joshua — the same name in Hebrew and Greek — who led Israel into Canaan. But Canaan was not the final rest; that God speaks, centuries later, of “another day” proves the earthly land was only a type and shadow of a better, spiritual, and abiding rest still to come.

The triumphant conclusion: there remains a Sabbath-rest for the people of God. grammatical-historical Gill and the expositors note that the word here changes — no longer the general rest but a sabbatismos, a Sabbath-keeping: a settled, sabbath-like repose of the soul in God, begun in grace and consummated in glory, the abiding inheritance of all who believe.

The one who has entered God's rest has himself rested from his works, as God did from his. grammatical-historical Gill takes the single figure chiefly of Christ, who, having finished the work of redemption, entered his own rest as God ceased from creation — yet the principle reaches every believer: to enter is to cease from the toil of self-justifying works and lean wholly on a finished work not our own. Gospel rest is a sabbath of the soul.

The paradox that gathers the argument: let us labour to enter that rest, lest anyone fall by the same pattern of unbelief. eastern Chrysostom insists that faith alone does not suffice — there must be a right life added, and much earnestness, to go up into heaven; grammatical-historical Robertson hears in spoudasōmen the call to be eager and alert; reformed Calvin reads “fall” as Moses' word for those who perished in the wilderness (Numbers 26:65) — to fall here is to perish. Strive, then, to enter a rest; the wilderness graves are the warning ever before us.

witnesses.point: Faith saves and none is saved without it, yet it does not suffice alone — a right life and much earnestness are needed to “labour to enter that rest.”
The rest believers enter now is a present, spiritual rest in Christ — soul-rest from the wearisome labour of seeking righteousness by the works of the law.
The fear urged here does not shake faith's confidence but guards against torpor; to “fall” is to perish, as the fathers fell in the wilderness.
Faith in the hearer is the life of the word; the same gospel was preached under both Testaments, but unbelief is the root of all unfruitfulness under it.

external.name: F.F. Bruce
external.point: The writer's coined word sabbatismos reaches beyond Canaan to a Sabbath-rest both presently entered by faith and finally consummated — the argument turning on David's later “Today” in Psalm 95.

1.application: “Strive to enter a rest” sounds like a contradiction until you see what it means: the striving is the ceasing — laying down the exhausting project of justifying yourself and resting on a finished work. The effort Christian faith asks is the effort to stop working for what Christ has already won.

12.exposition: These two verses stand like a drawn sword between the warning and the comfort. Having urged his readers to strive lest they fall by unbelief, the writer reminds them why no hidden apostasy is possible: the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged blade, cutting past every outward compliance to the very dividing-line of soul and spirit, laying open the heart's true motives. And behind the word stands its speaker: no creature is hidden from his sight; all lie naked before the eyes of the One to whom we must give account. The passage warns — you cannot fool the word, cannot escape the reckoning — but it also turns a corner, for the same all-seeing One is about to be named our sympathizing High Priest (v.14).
12.original_language: The word is zōn kai energēs — “living and active,” the old versions' “quick and powerful”; not a dead letter but a force at work. It is sharper than any two-edged sword and pierces where no blade reaches. The startling verb of v.13 is tetrachēlismena, literally “taken by the neck”: it evokes a wrestler bending back his opponent's head to bare the throat, or a sacrificial victim flayed open along the spine — total, defenceless exposure before the divine gaze. English “laid open” keeps the sense but loses the violence in it.
12.christ: Who is “the word of God” here? grammatical-historical Gill reads it of the personal Word — Christ himself, “the Word of God” being a known name of Messiah among the Jews, and the omniscient Lord “with whom we have to do” of v.13 the same divine person. puritan Matthew Henry grants that all this is true of the essential Word who “was God” (John 1:1), yet takes the primary sense to be the written and spoken word of God, quick and powerful when the Spirit sets it home. Either way the two verses meet in one Judge: the word that searches is the Christ before whom we stand.

verses.note: The word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and spirit, of joints and marrow, and discerning the thoughts and intentions of the heart. puritan Matthew Henry protests against calling it a dead letter: it is quick — living and life-giving — and powerful, cutting where no other blade can reach, severing from the soul the sinful habits grown as if natural to it, cutting off ignorance from the understanding and rebellion from the will. The blade turns the sinner's inside out and lets him see all that is in his heart.

No creature is hidden from his sight; all are naked and laid open to the eyes of him with whom we have to do. The verb is vivid — everything lies exposed as a victim flayed and split for sacrifice. puritan Matthew Henry catches the priestly image: as the high priest cut the sacrifice open to the backbone to see whether it was sound at heart, so all things lie dissected before the piercing eye of our great High Priest, who now tries our offerings and will at length try our state. Before such a Judge no hidden unbelief can shelter.

witnesses.point: He passes straight from the warning to the word — lest anyone think the penalty light, he sets before them the two-edged word that pierces soul and spirit.
“The word of God” here is Christ, the essential Word and omniscient Lord, before whom every creature lies open and manifest.
The word is no dead letter but living and life-giving; it severs the deep-rooted sinful habits of the soul and turns the sinner's inside out.

external.name: Harold W. Attridge
external.point: The passage personifies the divine word with escalating force, its vocabulary of piercing and exposure trading on sacrificial and even athletic imagery — the human person laid bare, throat exposed, before God.

12.application: The word that lays us bare is not our enemy. The God who sees every hidden motive also sees the faith no one else notices and the endurance no one else applauds. To be fully known by such a One is, in the end, the ground on which we dare to draw near.

14.exposition: Here the letter's central section opens, and the tone lifts from warning to encouragement. Two “therefores” frame it: because we have such a priest, let us hold fast our confession; and because he feels our weakness, let us draw near with boldness. The high priest is “great” — greater than Aaron — for he has passed not through a curtain but through the heavens themselves, and he is Jesus the Son of God, human and divine at once. His greatness is matched by his nearness: tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin, he can feel for the tempted as no distant deity could. And so the throne that might have been a seat of judgment is a throne of grace, and the trembling may come to it freely, sure of mercy and of timely help.
14.original_language: He is a high priest megas (“great”) who has dielēlythota tous ouranous — “passed through the heavens,” not merely into an earthly holy place. The verb of v.15, sympathēsai (“to suffer-with, to sympathize”), means more than pity: a fellow-feeling learned from within our trials. And the access of v.16 is meta parrēsias — “with boldness,” the frank confidence of a free citizen, not the cringing of a slave — coming to find eukairon boētheian, “well-timed help,” grace that arrives at the very hour of need.
14.historical_context: The contrast with Israel's worship is deliberate. The Aaronic high priest passed through a single veil once a year, into an earthly sanctuary, and came out again; and the high priests of the writer's own day were often political appointees, their office compromised. Jesus, by contrast, is “great” precisely because he has passed through the heavens themselves and remains there — a permanent priest in the true sanctuary, not a mortal repeating an annual ritual behind a curtain.
14.christ: The paired titles hold the whole letter together: “a great high priest” and “Jesus, the Son of God.” The Son whose throne is forever (1:8) is also the man tempted in every way we are. Neither the merely human nor the purely divine could serve: only the God-man can stand on God's side, having passed through the heavens, and on ours, having been where we are. His sinless endurance of temptation is what makes his sympathy trustworthy and his throne a throne of grace.

verses.note: The warning turns to comfort: since we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold fast our confession. grammatical-historical Gill names why he is a great priest — the dignity of his person, the worth of his sacrifice, and the place where he now serves, heaven itself, not a tent of skins. puritan Matthew Henry draws the contrast of the veil: the Aaronic priest passed once a year out of sight into the holiest; Christ has passed through the heavens to govern all, to send the Spirit, and to plead our cause. He is at once “Jesus” — a Saviour of our own nature — and “the Son of God” of divine dignity; therefore hold fast.

Yet this greatness is not remoteness: we have not a high priest unable to be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, but one tempted in every respect as we are, yet without sin. grammatical-historical Barnes insists he is not cold and unfeeling but abundantly qualified to feel for us, having himself borne our trials. The clause “yet without sin” does not diminish the sympathy but secures it: a priest who had yielded could offer only fallen solidarity, but one who met the whole range of temptation and never sinned offers both fellow-feeling and the authority to help — perfectly able to sympathize and perfectly holy.

The sweetest conclusion of all: let us then draw near with boldness to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need. puritan Matthew Henry marvels that God set up not a tribunal of inexorable justice but a throne of grace, where the chief of sinners may come — with reverence and godly fear, yet not dragged as to judgment but kindly invited to the mercy-seat. grammatical-historical Gill notes that it is the sympathizing High Priest, once typified by the mercy-seat, who makes such bold access possible: mercy to pardon our sins, and timely, seasonable grace for every hour of trial.

witnesses.point: Christ is a “great” high priest by the dignity of his person, the worth of his sacrifice, and his ministry in heaven itself, in distinction from the legal priests.
Our High Priest is not cold and unfeeling; having himself borne our trials, he is abundantly qualified to sympathize with us in our afflictions.
God set up not a tribunal of strict justice but a throne of grace; we come with reverence, yet invited to the mercy-seat, not dragged to judgment.

external.name: William L. Lane
external.point: These verses serve as the thesis statement for the central section on Christ's priesthood, the paired “let us hold fast” and “let us draw near” turning the whole preceding argument into pastoral appeal.

14.application: Boldness at God's throne rests on nothing in us and everything in him. Because our priest has felt exactly what we feel and never once gave way, we can carry the very weakness we are ashamed of straight to the throne — for that weakness, met and mastered in him, is the reason he is there.

_meta.title: The Rest That Remains, and a Great High Priest
reflection: The wilderness generation heard the same good news, yet it “did not profit them, not being mixed with faith” (v.2). Where are you hearing the word without letting faith mix with it?
To enter God's rest is to cease from your own works as he did from his (v.10), and yet we are told to “strive to enter” it (v.11). What self-justifying labour is God asking you to lay down?

Because our high priest was “tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin,” we may “draw near with boldness to the throne of grace” (vv.15–16). What weakness are you hiding that he invites you to bring to him instead?
